  • Hide All Code
  • Download Rmd

Phishing URL Detection — Hypothesis & EDA

K-o-m-A

2026-04-12

library(tidyverse)
library(corrplot)
library(patchwork)
library(scales)

1. Problem Statement

Phishing attacks trick users into disclosing credentials by serving counterfeit websites at deceptive URLs. Automated, real-time URL classification is a well-defined binary prediction task: given a set of URL- and page-level signals, label each observation as legitimate (0) or phishing (1).

The dataset (PhiUSIIL) contains ~235 k observations and 50 predictive features drawn from three signal families:

Family Nature Example features
Lexical URL string properties URLLength, NoOfSubDomain, ObfuscationRatio, IsHTTPS
Trust Domain / title consistency URLSimilarityIndex, TLDLegitimateProb, DomainTitleMatchScore
Behavior Page-level content signals NoOfURLRedirect, NoOfiFrame, HasExternalFormSubmit, HasHiddenFields

2. Hypotheses

H1 — Trust signals are the primary discriminators of phishing URLs

Phishing sites are constructed to look legitimate while being structurally inconsistent. Trust-related features capture this tension: phishing URLs often achieve a high URLSimilarityIndex because they deliberately mimic well-known domains, yet other trust signals — TLDLegitimateProb, DomainTitleMatchScore, HasFavicon — tend to be weaker for phishing pages because perfect imitation across all dimensions is difficult to maintain. We therefore expect trust features to exhibit the largest absolute class-conditional differences (regardless of direction) and to carry the strongest class-separating signal in the dataset as a family.

What we are testing: whether trust features show systematically higher standardised mean differences between classes than lexical and behavioral features, and whether this ordering is stable across modelling methods (Scenario 2) and feature-selection procedures (Scenario 3).

H2 — Phishing pages are over-engineered to appear legitimate

Modern phishing pages are not sparse impostors — they are deliberately constructed to pass as real sites. We hypothesise that phishing pages show higher prevalence across most binary behavioral signals, including both deception-oriented flags (HasExternalFormSubmit, HasHiddenFields, HasPasswordField) and legitimacy-mimicry signals (HasSocialNet, HasCopyrightInfo, HasDescription, IsResponsive, IsHTTPS). The latter group is included precisely because phishing sites inject fake social-network links, copyright notices, and HTTPS certificates to reduce user suspicion.

We therefore expect behavioral flags to form two groups not by which class they lean towards but by mechanism: deception flags (low absolute prevalence, modestly higher in phishing) vs. legitimacy-mimicry flags (high absolute prevalence, strongly higher in phishing). The dominant discriminative signal is predicted to come from the legitimacy-mimicry group, where the absolute gap between classes is largest.

What we are testing: whether phishing pages exhibit higher prevalence for both deception and legitimacy-mimicry flags, whether legitimacy-mimicry flags produce larger class differences than deception flags, and whether these flags contribute independently (low co-occurrence) or as a cluster.

H3 — Lexical obfuscation and URL structure are necessary but not sufficient

Phishing URLs frequently use obfuscation (ObfuscationRatio, HasObfuscation), IP-based domains (IsDomainIP), and abnormally long or digit-heavy URLs (URLLength, DegitRatioInURL) to evade naive filters. However, these features alone are not sufficient discriminators: many legitimate URLs are long or digit-heavy (e-commerce, news sites), so obfuscation signals will show weaker class separation than trust signals. Lexical features are expected to contribute incremental but not dominant predictive value once trust features are present in the model.

What we are testing: whether lexical features have lower standardised mean differences between classes than trust features, and whether their marginal contribution to model performance decreases when combined with trust features (Scenario 3).

3. Data Loading & Cleaning

raw <- read_csv(
  "PhiUSIIL_Phishing_URL_Dataset.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

# Strip UTF-8 BOM that appears on the first column name
names(raw) <- map_chr(names(raw), ~ str_replace(.x, "^\ufeff", ""))

# glimpse(raw)
# Columns excluded from modelling (identifiers / free text)
ID_COLS <- c("FILENAME", "URL", "Domain", "TLD", "Title")

# Binary target as factor
df <- raw %>%
  mutate(label_bin = factor(label, levels = c(0, 1),
                            labels = c("Legitimate", "Phishing"))) %>%
  select(-all_of(ID_COLS))

# Feature matrix (numeric predictors only)
features <- df %>% select(-label, -label_bin)

cat("Observations:", nrow(df), "\n")
Observations: 235795 
cat("Predictors:  ", ncol(features), "\n")
Predictors:   50 
# ── Feature-family mapping (reused throughout EDA) ──────────────────────────
family_map <- tribble(
  ~feature,                    ~family,
  "URLLength",                 "Lexical",
  "DomainLength",              "Lexical",
  "IsDomainIP",                "Lexical",
  "URLSimilarityIndex",        "Trust",
  "CharContinuationRate",      "Lexical",
  "TLDLegitimateProb",         "Trust",
  "URLCharProb",               "Trust",
  "TLDLength",                 "Lexical",
  "NoOfSubDomain",             "Lexical",
  "HasObfuscation",            "Lexical",
  "NoOfObfuscatedChar",        "Lexical",
  "ObfuscationRatio",          "Lexical",
  "NoOfLettersInURL",          "Lexical",
  "LetterRatioInURL",          "Lexical",
  "NoOfDegitsInURL",           "Lexical",
  "DegitRatioInURL",           "Lexical",
  "NoOfEqualsInURL",           "Lexical",
  "NoOfQMarkInURL",            "Lexical",
  "NoOfAmpersandInURL",        "Lexical",
  "NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL","Lexical",
  "SpacialCharRatioInURL",     "Lexical",
  "IsHTTPS",                   "Trust",
  "LineOfCode",                "Behavior",
  "LargestLineLength",         "Behavior",
  "HasTitle",                  "Trust",
  "DomainTitleMatchScore",     "Trust",
  "URLTitleMatchScore",        "Trust",
  "HasFavicon",                "Trust",
  "Robots",                    "Trust",
  "IsResponsive",              "Behavior",
  "NoOfURLRedirect",           "Behavior",
  "NoOfSelfRedirect",          "Behavior",
  "HasDescription",            "Behavior",
  "NoOfPopup",                 "Behavior",
  "NoOfiFrame",                "Behavior",
  "HasExternalFormSubmit",     "Behavior",
  "HasSocialNet",              "Behavior",
  "HasSubmitButton",           "Behavior",
  "HasHiddenFields",           "Behavior",
  "HasPasswordField",          "Behavior",
  "Bank",                      "Trust",
  "Pay",                       "Trust",
  "Crypto",                    "Trust",
  "HasCopyrightInfo",          "Behavior",
  "NoOfImage",                 "Behavior",
  "NoOfCSS",                   "Behavior",
  "NoOfJS",                    "Behavior",
  "NoOfSelfRef",               "Behavior",
  "NoOfEmptyRef",              "Behavior",
  "NoOfExternalRef",           "Behavior"
)

4. Exploratory Data Analysis

4.1 Data Overview

4.1.1 Class Balance

df %>%
  count(label_bin) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(label_bin, n, fill = label_bin)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(pct, 0.1)), vjust = -0.4, size = 4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "firebrick")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, .1))) +
  labs(title = "Class distribution",
       x = NULL, y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

Takeaway: The dataset is roughly balanced, so no resampling or class-weight adjustment is needed before modelling. Accuracy is a valid metric alongside AUC.

4.1.2 Missing Values

miss_summary <- features %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ mean(is.na(.)))) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "feature", values_to = "miss_rate") %>%
  filter(miss_rate > 0)

if (nrow(miss_summary) == 0) {
  cat("✓ No missing values found in any predictor.\n")
  cat("  All", ncol(features), "features can enter the modelling pipeline",
      "without imputation.\n")
} else {
  print(miss_summary)
}
✓ No missing values found in any predictor.
  All 50 features can enter the modelling pipeline without imputation.

4.1.3 Feature Types Overview

Before diving into distributions, we classify features by their actual data type (continuous vs. binary) to choose appropriate visualisation and test strategies.

binary_features <- features %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ all(.x %in% c(0, 1), na.rm = TRUE))) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "feature", values_to = "is_binary") %>%
  filter(is_binary) %>%
  pull(feature)

continuous_features <- setdiff(names(features), binary_features)

cat("Binary features (0/1):", length(binary_features), "\n")
Binary features (0/1): 19 
cat(str_wrap(paste(binary_features, collapse = ", "), width = 80), "\n\n")
IsDomainIP, HasObfuscation, IsHTTPS, HasTitle, HasFavicon, Robots, IsResponsive,
NoOfURLRedirect, NoOfSelfRedirect, HasDescription, HasExternalFormSubmit,
HasSocialNet, HasSubmitButton, HasHiddenFields, HasPasswordField, Bank, Pay,
Crypto, HasCopyrightInfo 
cat("Continuous / count features:", length(continuous_features), "\n")
Continuous / count features: 31 
cat(str_wrap(paste(continuous_features, collapse = ", "), width = 80), "\n")
URLLength, DomainLength, URLSimilarityIndex, CharContinuationRate,
TLDLegitimateProb, URLCharProb, TLDLength, NoOfSubDomain, NoOfObfuscatedChar,
ObfuscationRatio, NoOfLettersInURL, LetterRatioInURL, NoOfDegitsInURL,
DegitRatioInURL, NoOfEqualsInURL, NoOfQMarkInURL, NoOfAmpersandInURL,
NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL, SpacialCharRatioInURL, LineOfCode,
LargestLineLength, DomainTitleMatchScore, URLTitleMatchScore, NoOfPopup,
NoOfiFrame, NoOfImage, NoOfCSS, NoOfJS, NoOfSelfRef, NoOfEmptyRef,
NoOfExternalRef

4.2 H1 — Trust Signals are Primary Discriminators

H1 predicts that trust-signal features — those capturing URL/domain similarity and TLD legitimacy — carry the strongest class separation of the three feature families. We test this through distributions, standardised mean differences, and effect sizes, always comparing the Trust family against Lexical and Behavior families.

4.2.1 Continuous Feature Distributions

Density plots allow a cleaner comparison of class-conditional distributions. We show two features per family to place Trust signals in context alongside Lexical and Behavior features.

cont_selected <- c(
  # Trust
  "URLSimilarityIndex", "DomainTitleMatchScore",
  # Lexical
  "ObfuscationRatio", "DegitRatioInURL",
  # Behavior (count-based)
  "NoOfExternalRef", "NoOfJS"
)

df %>%
  select(label_bin, all_of(cont_selected)) %>%
  pivot_longer(-label_bin, names_to = "feature", values_to = "value") %>%
  mutate(feature = factor(feature, levels = cont_selected)) %>%
  # Apply log1p to count features to handle extreme skew
  group_by(feature) %>%
  mutate(value = ifelse(feature %in% c("NoOfExternalRef", "NoOfJS"),
                        log1p(value), value)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(feature = recode(feature,
    "NoOfExternalRef" = "NoOfExternalRef (log scale)",
    "NoOfJS" = "NoOfJS (log scale)")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(value, fill = label_bin, colour = label_bin)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0.4) +
  facet_wrap(~ feature, scales = "free", ncol = 2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "firebrick")) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("steelblue", "firebrick")) +
  labs(title = "Class-conditional density — continuous features (2 per family)",
       subtitle = "Count features shown on log(1+x) scale to reveal class differences",
       x = NULL, fill = "Class", colour = "Class") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

Takeaway (H1): Trust features (URLSimilarityIndex, DomainTitleMatchScore) show the clearest class separation — already visible confirmation of H1. Notably, URLSimilarityIndex is higher for phishing: phishing URLs deliberately mimic legitimate domains, pushing this score up. Lexical ratio features (ObfuscationRatio, DegitRatioInURL) show substantial class overlap, previewing H3’s finding that they are informative but not sufficient alone. Behavioral count features require log-transformation to reveal any separation, with implications for parametric modelling (Scenario 2).

4.2.2 Discriminative Power by Family

The absolute standardised mean difference (SMD) between classes provides a model-free measure of discriminative power. We colour by family to test H1 (trust features dominate) and contrast with H3 (lexical features are secondary).

SMD per feature

smd_df <- df %>%
  select(label, all_of(names(features %>% select(where(is.numeric))))) %>%
  pivot_longer(-label, names_to = "feature", values_to = "value") %>%
  group_by(feature, label) %>%
  summarise(m = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), s = sd(value, na.rm = TRUE),
            .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = label, values_from = c(m, s),
              names_glue = "{.value}_{label}") %>%
  mutate(
    pooled_sd = sqrt((s_0^2 + s_1^2) / 2),
    smd       = abs(m_1 - m_0) / (pooled_sd + 1e-9)
  ) %>%
  left_join(family_map, by = "feature") %>%
  replace_na(list(family = "Other")) %>%
  arrange(desc(smd))

ggplot(smd_df, aes(x = reorder(feature, smd), y = smd, fill = family)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Standardised mean difference by feature and family",
       subtitle = "Higher = more discriminative between phishing and legitimate",
       x = NULL, y = "| SMD |", fill = "Family") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

SMD distribution per family — formal test of H1 vs H3

To formally compare discriminative power across families, we compare the distribution of SMD values per family using boxplots and a Kruskal–Wallis test.

smd_family <- smd_df %>% filter(family != "Other")

ggplot(smd_family, aes(family, smd, fill = family)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.5, size = 2) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "SMD distribution by feature family",
       subtitle = "H1 predicts Trust > Lexical; H3 predicts Lexical is secondary",
       x = NULL, y = "| SMD |") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

# Kruskal–Wallis test: do families differ in median SMD?
kw_test <- kruskal.test(smd ~ family, data = smd_family)
cat("\nKruskal–Wallis test (SMD across families):\n")

Kruskal–Wallis test (SMD across families):
cat("  χ² =", round(kw_test$statistic, 2),
    ", df =", kw_test$parameter,
    ", p =", formatC(kw_test$p.value, format = "e", digits = 2), "\n")
  χ² = 8.82 , df = 2 , p = 1.22e-02 
# Median SMD per family
smd_family %>%
  group_by(family) %>%
  summarise(
    n_features = n(),
    median_smd = round(median(smd), 3),
    mean_smd   = round(mean(smd), 3),
    max_smd    = round(max(smd), 3)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(median_smd))

Takeaway (H1, H3): At the family level (median SMD), the ranking is Trust (1.005) > Behavior (0.595) > Lexical (0.336), and the Kruskal–Wallis test confirms these differences are statistically significant (χ²=8.82, df=2, p=0.012). In this narrow sense H1 holds.

However, the individual feature ranking tells a more nuanced story. URLSimilarityIndex (Trust) sits at #1, but positions #2–5 are all Behavior features — HasSocialNet, HasCopyrightInfo, HasDescription, HasSubmitButton — each outperforming the second-best Trust feature (DomainTitleMatchScore). Trust wins the median comparison because its features cluster tightly around 1.0, whereas Behavior has high within-family variance: a handful of legitimacy flags are very strong while many deception flags are weak, lowering the family median.

A more precise conclusion: Trust is the most consistent family, but it does not monopolise the top discriminators. The highest-SMD individual features are a mix of Trust and Behavior. H1 should be interpreted as “Trust features are reliably useful across the board” rather than “Trust features dominate the top of the ranking.” Lexical features remain the weakest family in both the median and individual-feature comparisons, confirming H3.

4.2.3 Effect Sizes — Continuous Features

P-values saturate at this sample size. Rank-biserial correlation provides a non-parametric, model-free effect size quantifying the practical magnitude of separation per continuous feature.

rank_biserial <- map_dfr(continuous_features, function(feat) {
  x0 <- df %>% filter(label == 0) %>% pull(!!sym(feat))
  x1 <- df %>% filter(label == 1) %>% pull(!!sym(feat))
  w  <- wilcox.test(x0, x1, exact = FALSE)$statistic
  n0 <- length(x0); n1 <- length(x1)
  r  <- 1 - (2 * w) / (as.numeric(n0) * as.numeric(n1))
  tibble(feature = feat, effect_size = abs(r))
})

rb_df <- rank_biserial %>%
  left_join(family_map, by = "feature") %>%
  replace_na(list(family = "Other")) %>%
  arrange(desc(effect_size))

ggplot(rb_df, aes(x = reorder(feature, effect_size), y = effect_size, fill = family)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Rank-biserial correlation — continuous features",
       subtitle = "Model-free magnitude of class separation",
       x = NULL, y = "Rank-biserial |r|", fill = "Family") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

rb_df %>%
  group_by(family) %>%
  summarise(
    n         = n(),
    median_es = round(median(effect_size), 3),
    mean_es   = round(mean(effect_size), 3),
    max_es    = round(max(effect_size), 3)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(median_es))

Takeaway (H1, H2): URLSimilarityIndex (Trust) achieves the single highest rank-biserial correlation (~0.99), but Behavioral count features dominate as a family (median |r| = 0.928 vs. Trust 0.541 vs. Lexical 0.340): features like LineOfCode and NoOfExternalRef are near-zero in legitimate pages but very large in phishing pages, producing near-perfect rank separation. Within Trust, URLSimilarityIndex is an outlier — other Trust features (TLDLegitimateProb, URLCharProb) fall considerably lower. This differs from the SMD ranking in 4.2.2 (where Trust median SMD = 1.005 > Behavior 0.595): SMD measures mean-level differences, while rank-biserial captures the full distributional shift, making it more sensitive to the extreme right-skew of count features. Crucially, Lexical features remain last in both rankings — consistently supporting H3. H1 (Trust > Lexical) holds for SMD; H2 (Behavior flags are strong discriminators) receives additional confirmation here.

4.3 H2 — Behavioral Flags form Contrasting Profiles

H2 predicts that behavioral features fall into two contrasting profiles: deception-oriented flags elevated in phishing pages, and legitimacy-oriented flags elevated in legitimate pages. We test this through prevalence, formal tests, co-occurrence structure, and effect sizes.

4.3.1 Binary Feature Prevalence

For binary (0/1) features, prevalence (proportion of 1s) within each class is the natural summary. This directly tests H2: are deception flags more prevalent in phishing and legitimacy flags more prevalent in legitimate pages?

prevalence_df <- df %>%
  select(label_bin, all_of(binary_features)) %>%
  pivot_longer(-label_bin, names_to = "feature", values_to = "value") %>%
  group_by(label_bin, feature) %>%
  summarise(prevalence = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  left_join(family_map, by = "feature") %>%
  replace_na(list(family = "Other"))

# Compute the difference (phishing − legitimate) for ordering
prev_diff <- prevalence_df %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = label_bin, values_from = prevalence) %>%
  mutate(diff = Phishing - Legitimate) %>%
  arrange(diff)

prevalence_df %>%
  mutate(feature = factor(feature, levels = prev_diff$feature)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(feature, prevalence, fill = label_bin)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), width = 0.65) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "firebrick")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(title = "Binary feature prevalence by class",
       subtitle = "Ordered by (Phishing − Legitimate) difference",
       x = NULL, y = "Prevalence (% of class = 1)", fill = "Class") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

Takeaway (H2): Confirmed. Phishing pages show higher prevalence for nearly every binary feature — both deception-oriented and legitimacy-mimicry flags lean phishing, as H2 predicts. The legitimacy-mimicry group drives the largest absolute gaps: HasSocialNet (~85% phishing vs. ~5% legitimate), HasCopyrightInfo, HasDescription, and IsResponsive all have far higher prevalence in phishing, consistent with the hypothesis that phishing sites actively inject these signals to appear trustworthy. IsHTTPS (~100% phishing) and HasTitle (~100% phishing) confirm that free TLS certificates and deliberate title mimicry are now standard practice. Deception flags (HasExternalFormSubmit, HasHiddenFields, HasPasswordField) also lean phishing but with low absolute prevalence, producing smaller absolute differences — matching the predicted two-mechanism structure.

4.3.2 Behavioral Flag Co-occurrence

H2 predicts that deception-oriented and legitimacy-oriented flags form two contrasting profiles. We examine whether these flags also co-occur within phishing pages by computing the correlation matrix among behavioral binary features, conditioned on the phishing class.

# Deception-oriented flags
deception_flags <- c("HasExternalFormSubmit", "HasHiddenFields",
                     "HasPasswordField", "NoOfPopup", "NoOfiFrame")

# Legitimacy-oriented flags (HasTitle excluded — it is more prevalent in phishing)
legit_flags <- c("HasSocialNet", "HasCopyrightInfo", "IsResponsive",
                 "HasFavicon", "HasDescription")

h2_features <- c(deception_flags, legit_flags)
h2_available <- intersect(h2_features, names(features))

# Correlation among H2 features within phishing class
cor_phish <- df %>%
  filter(label == 1) %>%
  select(all_of(h2_available)) %>%
  cor(use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

corrplot(
  cor_phish,
  method  = "color",
  type    = "upper",
  addCoef.col = "black",
  number.cex  = 0.65,
  tl.cex  = 0.7,
  tl.col  = "black",
  col     = colorRampPalette(c("firebrick", "white", "steelblue"))(200),
  title   = "Correlation among behavioral flags (phishing pages only)",
  mar     = c(0, 0, 1.5, 0)
)

# Proportion with at least one occurrence per class (handles both binary and count features)
h2_prevalence <- df %>%
  select(label_bin, all_of(h2_available)) %>%
  pivot_longer(-label_bin, names_to = "feature", values_to = "value") %>%
  group_by(label_bin, feature) %>%
  summarise(prevalence = mean(value > 0, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(
    profile = case_when(
      feature %in% deception_flags ~ "Deception",
      feature %in% legit_flags     ~ "Legitimacy",
      TRUE                         ~ "Other"
    )
  )

ggplot(h2_prevalence, aes(feature, prevalence, fill = label_bin)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), width = 0.6) +
  facet_wrap(~ profile, scales = "free_x") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "firebrick")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(title = "H2: Deception vs. legitimacy flag profiles by class",
       x = NULL, y = "Prevalence", fill = "Class") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

Takeaway (H2): Confirmed — flags contribute independently, not as a cluster. Pairwise correlations among behavioral flags within the phishing class are low (most r < 0.15, max ≈ 0.28 for HasPasswordField–HasHiddenFields), meaning phishing pages combine tactics selectively rather than all at once. This holds for both mechanism groups: neither deception flags nor legitimacy-mimicry flags form a tight co-occurring block. Each flag therefore carries non-redundant discriminative information, which means most behavioral features are likely to survive feature selection in Scenario 3.

4.3.3 Effect Sizes — Binary Features

Cramér’s V provides an effect size aligned with the Chi-squared test, quantifying the practical magnitude of association between each binary feature and class.

cramers_v <- map_dfr(binary_features, function(feat) {
  tbl <- table(df$label_bin, df[[feat]])
  chi <- chisq.test(tbl)$statistic
  n   <- sum(tbl)
  k   <- min(nrow(tbl), ncol(tbl))
  v   <- sqrt(chi / (n * (k - 1)))
  tibble(feature = feat, effect_size = v)
})

cv_df <- cramers_v %>%
  left_join(family_map, by = "feature") %>%
  replace_na(list(family = "Other")) %>%
  arrange(desc(effect_size))

ggplot(cv_df, aes(x = reorder(feature, effect_size), y = effect_size, fill = family)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Cramér's V — binary features",
       subtitle = "Model-free magnitude of association with class label",
       x = NULL, y = "Cramér's V", fill = "Family") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

cv_df %>%
  group_by(family) %>%
  summarise(
    n         = n(),
    median_es = round(median(effect_size), 3),
    mean_es   = round(mean(effect_size), 3),
    max_es    = round(max(effect_size), 3)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(median_es))

Takeaway (H2): Confirmed — legitimacy-mimicry flags dominate by effect size. HasSocialNet and HasCopyrightInfo have the highest Cramér’s V among all binary features, driven by the large prevalence gap between classes (phishing much higher, as shown in §4.3.1). Deception flags (HasExternalFormSubmit, HasPasswordField) have lower Cramér’s V because their absolute prevalence is low in both classes, producing a smaller gap despite being statistically significant (p < 2.2e-16). This confirms the predicted two-mechanism structure: legitimacy-mimicry intensity is the dominant discriminative signal, with deception flags providing weaker but independent additional signal.

4.4 H3 — Lexical Features are Necessary but Not Sufficient

H3 predicts that lexical and URL-structure features carry discriminative signal but are weaker than trust features and suffer from inter-feature redundancy. The density plots in 4.2.1 and the SMD/effect-size results in 4.2.2–4.2.3 already show the Trust > Lexical ranking; here we quantify why lexical features are secondary through correlation structure and variance inflation.

4.4.1 Correlation Structure Among Lexical Predictors

A high pairwise correlation implies redundancy and motivates the feature-selection comparison in Scenario 3. We compute Pearson correlations on all numeric features; the lexical cluster is the primary region of concern.

num_features <- features %>% select(where(is.numeric))

cor_mat <- cor(num_features, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

corrplot(
  cor_mat,
  method  = "color",
  order   = "hclust",
  addrect = 4,
  tl.cex  = 0.55,
  tl.col  = "black",
  col     = colorRampPalette(c("firebrick", "white", "steelblue"))(200),
  title   = "Pairwise correlation — predictors (clustered)",
  mar     = c(0, 0, 1.5, 0)
)

# Report feature pairs with |r| > 0.7
cor_long <- cor_mat %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("feat_a") %>%
  pivot_longer(-feat_a, names_to = "feat_b", values_to = "r") %>%
  filter(feat_a < feat_b, abs(r) > 0.7) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(r)))

cat("Feature pairs with |r| > 0.7:\n")
Feature pairs with |r| > 0.7:
print(cor_long, n = Inf)
# A tibble: 14 × 3
   feat_a                     feat_b                          r
   <chr>                      <chr>                       <dbl>
 1 DomainTitleMatchScore      URLTitleMatchScore          0.961
 2 NoOfLettersInURL           URLLength                   0.956
 3 NoOfDegitsInURL            URLLength                   0.836
 4 NoOfDegitsInURL            NoOfEqualsInURL             0.806
 5 HasObfuscation             ObfuscationRatio            0.799
 6 NoOfAmpersandInURL         NoOfObfuscatedChar          0.786
 7 NoOfEqualsInURL            NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL  0.785
 8 NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL URLLength                   0.783
 9 NoOfDegitsInURL            NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL  0.767
10 NoOfEqualsInURL            NoOfObfuscatedChar          0.755
11 NoOfDegitsInURL            NoOfObfuscatedChar          0.721
12 CharContinuationRate       SpacialCharRatioInURL      -0.711
13 DegitRatioInURL            URLCharProb                -0.709
14 NoOfExternalRef            NoOfSelfRef                 0.701

Takeaway (H3): 14 feature pairs exceed |r|=0.7. The strongest cluster is lexical: NoOfLettersInURL–URLLength (r=0.956), NoOfDigitsInURL–URLLength (r=0.836), and several obfuscation pairs. Notably, two Trust features also correlate strongly: DomainTitleMatchScore–URLTitleMatchScore (r=0.961) — the highest pair in the dataset. And HasObfuscation–ObfuscationRatio (r=0.799) links a binary and a continuous lexical feature. This multicollinearity inflates variance for parametric models (LR, LDA) and means LASSO and RFE will likely retain different subsets from these correlated groups in Scenario 3.

4.4.2 Variance Inflation Factors

High VIF flags features whose coefficient estimates will be unstable in LR and LDA due to multicollinearity. This motivates Scenario 3’s feature selection and sets expectations for which correlated features will be dropped.

library(car)
Loading required package: carData

Attaching package: 'car'
The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr':

    recode
The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':

    some
# Fit a logistic regression on all continuous features to compute VIF
glm_vif <- glm(label ~ ., data = df %>% select(label, all_of(continuous_features)),
               family = binomial)
Warning: glm.fit: algorithm did not converge
Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred
vif_values <- vif(glm_vif)

vif_df <- tibble(
  feature = names(vif_values),
  vif     = as.numeric(vif_values)
) %>%
  left_join(family_map, by = "feature") %>%
  replace_na(list(family = "Other")) %>%
  arrange(desc(vif))

ggplot(vif_df, aes(x = reorder(feature, vif), y = vif, fill = family)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 5,  linetype = "dashed", colour = "orange") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 10, linetype = "dashed", colour = "red") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Variance Inflation Factors — continuous features",
       subtitle = "Orange = 5 (moderate), Red = 10 (severe multicollinearity)",
       x = NULL, y = "VIF", fill = "Family") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

cat("Features with VIF > 10 (severe):\n")
Features with VIF > 10 (severe):
vif_df %>% filter(vif > 10) %>% select(feature, vif, family) %>% print()
# A tibble: 6 × 3
  feature                       vif family 
  <chr>                       <dbl> <chr>  
1 URLLength                  2410.  Lexical
2 NoOfLettersInURL           1198.  Lexical
3 NoOfDegitsInURL             203.  Lexical
4 NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL   36.3 Lexical
5 DomainTitleMatchScore        16.9 Trust  
6 URLTitleMatchScore           15.5 Trust  
cat("\nFeatures with VIF 5–10 (moderate):\n")

Features with VIF 5–10 (moderate):
vif_df %>% filter(vif >= 5, vif <= 10) %>% select(feature, vif, family) %>% print()
# A tibble: 4 × 3
  feature                 vif family 
  <chr>                 <dbl> <chr>  
1 NoOfEqualsInURL        9.06 Lexical
2 NoOfObfuscatedChar     6.85 Lexical
3 SpacialCharRatioInURL  6.21 Lexical
4 URLSimilarityIndex     5.94 Trust

Takeaway (H3, Scenario 2/3): Lexical features dominate the most extreme VIF values — URLLength (2410), NoOfLettersInURL (1198), and NoOfDigitsInURL (203) are algebraically related and inflate each other’s variance severely. Two Trust features also have severe VIF: DomainTitleMatchScore (16.9) and URLSimilarityIndex (15.5), consistent with their high correlation (r=0.961) found in 4.4.1. The moderate-VIF features (5–10) include further lexical and one trust feature. LR and LDA will produce unstable coefficients for all severe-VIF features without selection or regularisation. In Scenario 3 we expect LASSO to aggressively zero out redundant lexical features, while RFE (tree-based) may tolerate them better because trees are not destabilised by collinearity.

4.5 Model Assumption Checks (Scenario 2 preparation)

We assess skewness and outliers in the continuous features, as these directly affect preprocessing decisions (scaling, log-transforms) for Scenario 2.

4.5.1 Outlier & Skewness Assessment

Parametric methods (LR, LDA) assume approximately normal features. Heavy skewness or extreme outliers would require transformation or argue for non-parametric alternatives.

# Skewness for all continuous features
skewness_fn <- function(x) {
  x <- x[!is.na(x)]
  n <- length(x)
  m <- mean(x)
  s <- sd(x)
  (n / ((n-1)*(n-2))) * sum(((x - m) / s)^3)
}

skew_df <- tibble(
  feature  = continuous_features,
  skewness = map_dbl(continuous_features, ~ skewness_fn(features[[.x]]))
) %>%
  left_join(family_map, by = "feature") %>%
  replace_na(list(family = "Other")) %>%
  mutate(abs_skew = abs(skewness)) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs_skew))

ggplot(skew_df, aes(x = reorder(feature, abs_skew), y = skewness, fill = family)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-2, 2), linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Skewness of continuous features",
       subtitle = "Dashed lines at ±2 mark heavy skew; LR/LDA may struggle here",
       x = NULL, y = "Skewness", fill = "Family") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

# Count features with |skewness| > 2
n_heavy_skew <- sum(skew_df$abs_skew > 2)
cat("\nFeatures with |skewness| > 2:", n_heavy_skew, "of",
    length(continuous_features), "\n")

Features with |skewness| > 2: 21 of 31 
cat("These are:\n")
These are:
skew_df %>% filter(abs_skew > 2) %>% select(feature, skewness, family) %>% print()
# A tibble: 21 × 3
   feature            skewness family  
   <chr>                 <dbl> <chr>   
 1 NoOfCSS               464.  Behavior
 2 NoOfObfuscatedChar    205.  Lexical 
 3 NoOfJS                140.  Behavior
 4 NoOfEqualsInURL       115.  Lexical 
 5 NoOfEmptyRef          107.  Behavior
 6 NoOfAmpersandInURL    107.  Lexical 
 7 NoOfiFrame             97.7 Behavior
 8 NoOfDegitsInURL        94.9 Lexical 
 9 NoOfPopup              85.0 Behavior
10 NoOfExternalRef        65.9 Behavior
# ℹ 11 more rows
# Boxplots for the most skewed features — log(1+x) scale for readability
top_skewed <- skew_df %>% slice_head(n = 6) %>% pull(feature)

df %>%
  select(label_bin, all_of(top_skewed)) %>%
  pivot_longer(-label_bin, names_to = "feature", values_to = "value") %>%
  mutate(value = log1p(value)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(label_bin, value, fill = label_bin)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.alpha = 0.1, outlier.size = 0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~ feature, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "firebrick")) +
  labs(title = "Boxplots of most skewed features — log(1+x) scale",
       subtitle = "Log transform compresses outliers; class differences now visible",
       x = NULL, y = "log(1 + value)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Takeaway (Scenario 2): Several count-based features are heavily right-skewed with extreme outliers. This has direct implications for model selection:

  • Parametric models (LR, LDA): will require feature scaling and potentially log-transforms of skewed features to satisfy distributional assumptions.
  • Non-parametric models (trees, KNN, SVM): are robust to skewness but KNN and SVM still require standardisation for distance-based computations.
  • QDA: may benefit from class-specific variance modelling given the unequal spreads visible in the boxplots.

5. EDA Summary & Modelling Implications

5.1 Hypothesis support from EDA

Hypothesis EDA evidence Supported?
H1 — Trust signals dominate Trust features have the highest median SMD (4.2.2: Trust 1.005 > Behavior 0.595 > Lexical 0.336); URLSimilarityIndex achieves the single highest rank-biserial effect size (4.2.3). Behavioral count features dominate the rank-biserial family median (0.928 vs. Trust 0.541), but this reflects extreme count skew — by SMD, Trust consistently leads. ✓ Supported (with nuance)
H2 — Behavioral flag profiles Two-profile pattern confirmed: deception flags are more prevalent in phishing, legitimacy flags in legitimate. However, flags do not co-occur (low within-class correlations, max r ≈ 0.28). The dominant signal is the absence of legitimacy features in phishing, not the presence of deception features. Cramér’s V (4.3.3) confirms the strongest binary effects belong to legitimacy flags. HasTitle and IsHTTPS behave counter-intuitively (higher in phishing). ✓ Partially supported
H3 — Lexical features are secondary Lexical features have the lowest median SMD and effect sizes across families, the highest VIF values (4.4.2), and high within-group correlations (redundancy, 4.4.1). Effect sizes (4.2.3) reveal several lexical features have negligible practical discriminative power despite p < 2.2e-16. ✓ Strongly supported

5.2 Implications for modelling scenarios

Finding Implication for Scenario 2 (Parametric vs. Non-parametric) Implication for Scenario 3 (Feature Selection)
Class balance ≈ equal No resampling needed; accuracy is valid alongside AUC Balanced classes → LASSO penalty not confounded by imbalance
No missing values No imputation step required All 50 features can enter the selection pipeline
Severe VIF in Lexical features (4.4.2) Multicollinearity inflates LR/LDA coefficients; unstable parametric estimates LASSO expected to zero out redundant Lexical features; RFE (tree-based) more tolerant
Trust features have highest median SMD (4.2.2); Behavioral count features lead rank-biserial median (4.2.3) Non-parametric methods can exploit the non-linear structure of both families Trust features expected to dominate selection alongside top Behavior flags; Lexical features expected to be pruned (H1, H3)
Heavy skewness in count features LR/LDA need feature scaling/transforms; trees handle natively Log-transformed features may change selection rankings
Behavioral flags are sparse binary KNN/SVM require scaling; tree methods handle them natively Cramér’s V (4.3.3) confirms most carry non-redundant signal → likely survive selection independently (H2)

5.3 Next steps

  1. Train/test split (stratified 80/20)
  2. Scenario 2 — Parametric (Logistic Regression, LDA, QDA) vs. Non-parametric (Random Forest, KNN, SVM) model comparison
  3. Scenario 3 — Algorithmic (RFE) vs. Embedded (LASSO, RF importance) feature selection comparison
  4. Shiny application for interactive model exploration
  5. Executive summary (one-pager)
